rationale.R1.1: The artifact contains a thesis claim that shifts mid-artifact: the opening frames SQL accuracy as the central contribution, but the final paragraph reassigns load-bearing status to schema documentation generation. Reconstructing whether one or two contribution claims are being made is central to any substantive review.
rationale.R1.2: There is a visible inferential leap: the Cost Auditor filters high-cost plans, and from this the artifact concludes that queries are 'more readable to human DBAs.' Logical consistency work is needed to evaluate whether that inference is valid or whether readability and cost are being conflated.
rationale.R1.3: Multiple empirical claims are present (71.4% vs 64.2% accuracy, 4.1/5 readability rating, 68% documentation agreement), and the evidentiary basis for each varies. The readability claim in particular appears to rest on the cost-filtering mechanism rather than the user-study result, suggesting a potential evidence-claim mismatch.
rationale.R1.6: The phrasing 'take a look at this paragraph' is a writing-review trigger idiom and could mean the user wants prose-level feedback. Writing quality is therefore centrally expected regardless of whether deeper analytical review is also requested.

parsed.clarification_question: Should I focus on improving the prose and sentence-level writing quality, or on critically evaluating the logical and empirical claims (e.g., the shifted contribution framing and the readability inference)?
